According to shrine tradition, Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto, ruler of the land of night, dispatched Tajikitsuhime-no-Mikoto to Ōtaki-yama as an inspector toward {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}, and from here had her watch over the {{ill|lt=Seto Inland Sea|Q231312|WD=Q231312|1=Seto Inland Sea}}; this is said to be the shrine's origin. It is said that in the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} practiced ascetic training on this mountain and installed Nishiteru Daigongen, making it a mountain training site of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=UNKNOWN}}. Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, it was called "Nishiteru Gongen." In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was venerated by the Inada clan, {{ill|lt=chief retainers|Q1316445|WD=Q1316445|1=Karō}} of the {{ill|lt=Tokushima Domain|Q3034557|WD=Q3034557|1=Tokushima Domain}}, who donated 9 chō 8 tan of shrine land, and by the lords of the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}}, who donated 50 koku for operating expenses as their prayer site. In the early Meiji era, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu bunri edict|1=UNKNOWN}} separated the shrine from its temple ({{ill|lt=Ōtaki-ji|Q11437672|WD=Q11437672|1=Otaki-ji Temple}}). In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was renamed Nishiteru Shrine and ranked as gōsha. * Legend of the komainu: The shrine has a {{ill|lt=komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} dedicated in {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 14 ({{ill|lt=1843|Q7629|WD=Q7629|1=1843}}). One summer night, a mother and child passing here were chased by a {{ill|lt=wolf|Q18498|WD=Q18498|lt=wolf|1=Wolf}} and fled into the shrine. When the wolf tried to attack them, the komainu drove it away. Due to age, the old statue was moved to a safe place under the honden, and a new pair now stands at the former position by the stone steps. ===Precincts=== * First torii: Beside the road; stone steps continue from here into the precincts. * Shrine office, kagura hall, and plaza * Second torii * Main shrine: haiden and honden * Inari Shrine: deity is Uga-no-Mitama Ōkami. Haiden and honden are combined in one structure. * Kumano Shrine: deities are Kumano Gongen, Izanagi Ōkami, and Izanami Ōkami. The Daigongen formerly enshrined at Okunoin of Mt. Ōtaki was moved here in early Shōwa. * Hachidai Ryūō Shrine: deities are Nanda, Upananda, Sāgara, Vāsuki, Takṣaka, Anavatapta, Manasvin, and Utpalaka. Stone hokora. * Prayer-stone area and votive horse painting "Tsuki no Mokuba" (painted by Fukuke Haruyo; completion ceremony 2019-11-10) * Tōmei cedar: height 50 m, said to be 500 years old and known as a "thousand-year cedar." Tradition says the tip shone nightly at the start of the First Sino-Japanese War and went dark at peace talks; a similar event is also said for the Russo-Japanese War. A parking lot is on the east pass side past Ōtaki-ji on Prefectural Route 106. A pair of komainu was installed at the east entrance in July 2025; walking about 50 m along the approach from there leads into the precincts. The above shrine buildings line the crest running east from the summit one level above the shrine-office plaza. ===Images=== Nisiteru shirine 03.jpg|Stone steps from the prefectural road to the precincts Nisiteru shirine 02.jpg|Main shrine haiden Nishiteru Jinja 05.JPG|Honden Nisiteru shirine 20240706 2.jpg|Kagura hall Nisiteru_jinja_20191217_02.jpg|Inari Shrine Nisiteruzinzya 01.jpg|Kumano Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 1.jpg|Hachidai Ryūō Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 2.jpg|Old komainu Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 3.jpg|Precincts Nisiteru 20250823 1.jpg|East entrance === References === {{No footnotes|date=August 2020|section=1}} * Tokushima Historical Society (ed.), ''New Edition: Historical Walks of Tokushima Prefecture'', Yamakawa Publishing, 1995, pp. 68-69. * On-site explanatory board === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commons|Category:Nishiteru-jinja}} *[http://nisiteru-jinja.com/ Mt. Ōtaki Nishiteru Shrine] - official site == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:徳島県の神社]] [[Category:阿波国の式内社]] [[Category:美馬市の歴史]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:美馬市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

{{wikidata link|Q11629158}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Nishiteru Shrine | 画像=[[File:Nisiteru shirine 01.jpg|270px]]
Main shrine haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}Wakimachi Nishiotani 672 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭= | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Tokushima|label=Nishiteru Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nishiteru Shrine'''（にしてるじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Wakimachi (Mima)|Q28689983|WD=Q28689983|lt=Wakimachi|1=Wakimachi}}, {{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}, {{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and is also called "Tsukigami-no-miya" (Shrine of the Moon Deity). It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. Under the former {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. The main deity is Tsukuyomi Ōkami ({{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}), with the three Munakata goddesses as associated deities: {{ill|lt=Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|WD=Q10932592|lt=Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagitsuhime}}, {{ill|lt=Takiribime|Q10932870|WD=Q10932870|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagorihime}}, and {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}. === Overview === It is located directly below the summit (946{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}) of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki-yama (Sanuki Mountains)|Q11437573|WD=Q11437573|lt=Ōtaki-yama|1=Mount Otaki}}, on the border of {{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}, Tokushima Prefecture and {{ill|lt=Takamatsu|Q200707|WD=Q200707|1=Takamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is treated as a candidate shrine for Tajiki Shrine listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. According to shrine tradition, Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto, ruler of the land of night, dispatched Tajikitsuhime-no-Mikoto to Ōtaki-yama as an inspector toward {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}, and from here had her watch over the {{ill|lt=Seto Inland Sea|Q231312|WD=Q231312|1=Seto Inland Sea}}; this is said to be the shrine's origin. It is said that in the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} practiced ascetic training on this mountain and installed Nishiteru Daigongen, making it a mountain training site of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=UNKNOWN}}. Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, it was called "Nishiteru Gongen." In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was venerated by the Inada clan, {{ill|lt=chief retainers|Q1316445|WD=Q1316445|1=Karō}} of the {{ill|lt=Tokushima Domain|Q3034557|WD=Q3034557|1=Tokushima Domain}}, who donated 9 chō 8 tan of shrine land, and by the lords of the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}}, who donated 50 koku for operating expenses as their prayer site. In the early Meiji era, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu bunri edict|1=UNKNOWN}} separated the shrine from its temple ({{ill|lt=Ōtaki-ji|Q11437672|WD=Q11437672|1=Otaki-ji Temple}}). In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was renamed Nishiteru Shrine and ranked as gōsha. * Legend of the komainu: The shrine has a {{ill|lt=komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} dedicated in {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 14 ({{ill|lt=1843|Q7629|WD=Q7629|1=1843}}). One summer night, a mother and child passing here were chased by a {{ill|lt=wolf|Q18498|WD=Q18498|lt=wolf|1=Wolf}} and fled into the shrine. When the wolf tried to attack them, the komainu drove it away. Due to age, the old statue was moved to a safe place under the honden, and a new pair now stands at the former position by the stone steps. ===Precincts=== * First torii: Beside the road; stone steps continue from here into the precincts. * Shrine office, kagura hall, and plaza * Second torii * Main shrine: haiden and honden * Inari Shrine: deity is Uga-no-Mitama Ōkami. Haiden and honden are combined in one structure. * Kumano Shrine: deities are Kumano Gongen, Izanagi Ōkami, and Izanami Ōkami. The Daigongen formerly enshrined at Okunoin of Mt. Ōtaki was moved here in early Shōwa. * Hachidai Ryūō Shrine: deities are Nanda, Upananda, Sāgara, Vāsuki, Takṣaka, Anavatapta, Manasvin, and Utpalaka. Stone hokora. * Prayer-stone area and votive horse painting "Tsuki no Mokuba" (painted by Fukuke Haruyo; completion ceremony 2019-11-10) * Tōmei cedar: height 50 m, said to be 500 years old and known as a "thousand-year cedar." Tradition says the tip shone nightly at the start of the First Sino-Japanese War and went dark at peace talks; a similar event is also said for the Russo-Japanese War. A parking lot is on the east pass side past Ōtaki-ji on Prefectural Route 106. A pair of komainu was installed at the east entrance in July 2025; walking about 50 m along the approach from there leads into the precincts. The above shrine buildings line the crest running east from the summit one level above the shrine-office plaza. ===Images=== Nisiteru shirine 03.jpg|Stone steps from the prefectural road to the precincts Nisiteru shirine 02.jpg|Main shrine haiden Nishiteru Jinja 05.JPG|Honden Nisiteru shirine 20240706 2.jpg|Kagura hall Nisiteru_jinja_20191217_02.jpg|Inari Shrine Nisiteruzinzya 01.jpg|Kumano Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 1.jpg|Hachidai Ryūō Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 2.jpg|Old komainu Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 3.jpg|Precincts Nisiteru 20250823 1.jpg|East entrance === References === {{No footnotes|date=August 2020|section=1}} * Tokushima Historical Society (ed.), ''New Edition: Historical Walks of Tokushima Prefecture'', Yamakawa Publishing, 1995, pp. 68-69. * On-site explanatory board === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commons|Category:Nishiteru-jinja}} *[http://nisiteru-jinja.com/ Mt. Ōtaki Nishiteru Shrine] - official site == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:阿波国の式内社]] [[Category:美馬市の歴史]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:美馬市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

{{wikidata link|Q11629158}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Nishiteru Shrine | 画像=[[File:Nisiteru shirine 01.jpg|270px]]
Main shrine haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}Wakimachi Nishiotani 672 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭= | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Tokushima|label=Nishiteru Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nishiteru Shrine'''（にしてるじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Wakimachi (Mima)|Q28689983|WD=Q28689983|lt=Wakimachi|1=Wakimachi}}, {{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}, {{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and is also called "Tsukigami-no-miya" (Shrine of the Moon Deity). It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. Under the former {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. The main deity is Tsukuyomi Ōkami ({{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}), with the three Munakata goddesses as associated deities: {{ill|lt=Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|WD=Q10932592|lt=Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagitsuhime}}, {{ill|lt=Takiribime|Q10932870|WD=Q10932870|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagorihime}}, and {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}. === Overview === It is located directly below the summit (946{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}) of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki-yama (Sanuki Mountains)|Q11437573|WD=Q11437573|lt=Ōtaki-yama|1=Mount Otaki}}, on the border of {{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}, Tokushima Prefecture and {{ill|lt=Takamatsu|Q200707|WD=Q200707|1=Takamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is treated as a candidate shrine for Tajiki Shrine listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. According to shrine tradition, Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto, ruler of the land of night, dispatched Tajikitsuhime-no-Mikoto to Ōtaki-yama as an inspector toward {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}, and from here had her watch over the {{ill|lt=Seto Inland Sea|Q231312|WD=Q231312|1=Seto Inland Sea}}; this is said to be the shrine's origin. It is said that in the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} practiced ascetic training on this mountain and installed Nishiteru Daigongen, making it a mountain training site of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=UNKNOWN}}. Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, it was called "Nishiteru Gongen." In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was venerated by the Inada clan, {{ill|lt=chief retainers|Q1316445|WD=Q1316445|1=Karō}} of the {{ill|lt=Tokushima Domain|Q3034557|WD=Q3034557|1=Tokushima Domain}}, who donated 9 chō 8 tan of shrine land, and by the lords of the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}}, who donated 50 koku for operating expenses as their prayer site. In the early Meiji era, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu bunri edict|1=UNKNOWN}} separated the shrine from its temple ({{ill|lt=Ōtaki-ji|Q11437672|WD=Q11437672|1=Otaki-ji Temple}}). In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was renamed Nishiteru Shrine and ranked as gōsha. * Legend of the komainu: The shrine has a {{ill|lt=komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} dedicated in {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 14 ({{ill|lt=1843|Q7629|WD=Q7629|1=1843}}). One summer night, a mother and child passing here were chased by a {{ill|lt=wolf|Q18498|WD=Q18498|lt=wolf|1=Wolf}} and fled into the shrine. When the wolf tried to attack them, the komainu drove it away. Due to age, the old statue was moved to a safe place under the honden, and a new pair now stands at the former position by the stone steps. ===Precincts=== * First torii: Beside the road; stone steps continue from here into the precincts. * Shrine office, kagura hall, and plaza * Second torii * Main shrine: haiden and honden * Inari Shrine: deity is Uga-no-Mitama Ōkami. Haiden and honden are combined in one structure. * Kumano Shrine: deities are Kumano Gongen, Izanagi Ōkami, and Izanami Ōkami. The Daigongen formerly enshrined at Okunoin of Mt. Ōtaki was moved here in early Shōwa. * Hachidai Ryūō Shrine: deities are Nanda, Upananda, Sāgara, Vāsuki, Takṣaka, Anavatapta, Manasvin, and Utpalaka. Stone hokora. * Prayer-stone area and votive horse painting "Tsuki no Mokuba" (painted by Fukuke Haruyo; completion ceremony 2019-11-10) * Tōmei cedar: height 50 m, said to be 500 years old and known as a "thousand-year cedar." Tradition says the tip shone nightly at the start of the First Sino-Japanese War and went dark at peace talks; a similar event is also said for the Russo-Japanese War. A parking lot is on the east pass side past Ōtaki-ji on Prefectural Route 106. A pair of komainu was installed at the east entrance in July 2025; walking about 50 m along the approach from there leads into the precincts. The above shrine buildings line the crest running east from the summit one level above the shrine-office plaza. ===Images=== Nisiteru shirine 03.jpg|Stone steps from the prefectural road to the precincts Nisiteru shirine 02.jpg|Main shrine haiden Nishiteru Jinja 05.JPG|Honden Nisiteru shirine 20240706 2.jpg|Kagura hall Nisiteru_jinja_20191217_02.jpg|Inari Shrine Nisiteruzinzya 01.jpg|Kumano Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 1.jpg|Hachidai Ryūō Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 2.jpg|Old komainu Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 3.jpg|Precincts Nisiteru 20250823 1.jpg|East entrance === References === {{No footnotes|date=August 2020|section=1}} * Tokushima Historical Society (ed.), ''New Edition: Historical Walks of Tokushima Prefecture'', Yamakawa Publishing, 1995, pp. 68-69. * On-site explanatory board === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commons|Category:Nishiteru-jinja}} *[http://nisiteru-jinja.com/ Mt. Ōtaki Nishiteru Shrine] - official site == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:美馬市の歴史]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:美馬市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

Main shrine haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}Wakimachi Nishiotani 672 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭= | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Tokushima|label=Nishiteru Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nishiteru Shrine'''（にしてるじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Wakimachi (Mima)|Q28689983|WD=Q28689983|lt=Wakimachi|1=Wakimachi}}, {{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}, {{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and is also called "Tsukigami-no-miya" (Shrine of the Moon Deity). It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. Under the former {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. The main deity is Tsukuyomi Ōkami ({{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}), with the three Munakata goddesses as associated deities: {{ill|lt=Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|WD=Q10932592|lt=Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagitsuhime}}, {{ill|lt=Takiribime|Q10932870|WD=Q10932870|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagorihime}}, and {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}. === Overview === It is located directly below the summit (946{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}) of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki-yama (Sanuki Mountains)|Q11437573|WD=Q11437573|lt=Ōtaki-yama|1=Mount Otaki}}, on the border of {{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}, Tokushima Prefecture and {{ill|lt=Takamatsu|Q200707|WD=Q200707|1=Takamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is treated as a candidate shrine for Tajiki Shrine listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. According to shrine tradition, Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto, ruler of the land of night, dispatched Tajikitsuhime-no-Mikoto to Ōtaki-yama as an inspector toward {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}, and from here had her watch over the {{ill|lt=Seto Inland Sea|Q231312|WD=Q231312|1=Seto Inland Sea}}; this is said to be the shrine's origin. It is said that in the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} practiced ascetic training on this mountain and installed Nishiteru Daigongen, making it a mountain training site of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=UNKNOWN}}. Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, it was called "Nishiteru Gongen." In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was venerated by the Inada clan, {{ill|lt=chief retainers|Q1316445|WD=Q1316445|1=Karō}} of the {{ill|lt=Tokushima Domain|Q3034557|WD=Q3034557|1=Tokushima Domain}}, who donated 9 chō 8 tan of shrine land, and by the lords of the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}}, who donated 50 koku for operating expenses as their prayer site. In the early Meiji era, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu bunri edict|1=UNKNOWN}} separated the shrine from its temple ({{ill|lt=Ōtaki-ji|Q11437672|WD=Q11437672|1=Otaki-ji Temple}}). In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was renamed Nishiteru Shrine and ranked as gōsha. * Legend of the komainu: The shrine has a {{ill|lt=komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} dedicated in {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 14 ({{ill|lt=1843|Q7629|WD=Q7629|1=1843}}). One summer night, a mother and child passing here were chased by a {{ill|lt=wolf|Q18498|WD=Q18498|lt=wolf|1=Wolf}} and fled into the shrine. When the wolf tried to attack them, the komainu drove it away. Due to age, the old statue was moved to a safe place under the honden, and a new pair now stands at the former position by the stone steps. ===Precincts=== * First torii: Beside the road; stone steps continue from here into the precincts. * Shrine office, kagura hall, and plaza * Second torii * Main shrine: haiden and honden * Inari Shrine: deity is Uga-no-Mitama Ōkami. Haiden and honden are combined in one structure. * Kumano Shrine: deities are Kumano Gongen, Izanagi Ōkami, and Izanami Ōkami. The Daigongen formerly enshrined at Okunoin of Mt. Ōtaki was moved here in early Shōwa. * Hachidai Ryūō Shrine: deities are Nanda, Upananda, Sāgara, Vāsuki, Takṣaka, Anavatapta, Manasvin, and Utpalaka. Stone hokora. * Prayer-stone area and votive horse painting "Tsuki no Mokuba" (painted by Fukuke Haruyo; completion ceremony 2019-11-10) * Tōmei cedar: height 50 m, said to be 500 years old and known as a "thousand-year cedar." Tradition says the tip shone nightly at the start of the First Sino-Japanese War and went dark at peace talks; a similar event is also said for the Russo-Japanese War. A parking lot is on the east pass side past Ōtaki-ji on Prefectural Route 106. A pair of komainu was installed at the east entrance in July 2025; walking about 50 m along the approach from there leads into the precincts. The above shrine buildings line the crest running east from the summit one level above the shrine-office plaza. ===Images=== Nisiteru shirine 03.jpg|Stone steps from the prefectural road to the precincts Nisiteru shirine 02.jpg|Main shrine haiden Nishiteru Jinja 05.JPG|Honden Nisiteru shirine 20240706 2.jpg|Kagura hall Nisiteru_jinja_20191217_02.jpg|Inari Shrine Nisiteruzinzya 01.jpg|Kumano Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 1.jpg|Hachidai Ryūō Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 2.jpg|Old komainu Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 3.jpg|Precincts Nisiteru 20250823 1.jpg|East entrance === References === {{No footnotes|date=August 2020|section=1}} * Tokushima Historical Society (ed.), ''New Edition: Historical Walks of Tokushima Prefecture'', Yamakawa Publishing, 1995, pp. 68-69. * On-site explanatory board === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commons|Category:Nishiteru-jinja}} *[http://nisiteru-jinja.com/ Mt. Ōtaki Nishiteru Shrine] - official site == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:美馬市の歴史]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:美馬市の建築物]]

A parking lot is on the east pass side past Ōtaki-ji on Prefectural Route 106. A pair of komainu was installed at the east entrance in July 2025; walking about 50 m along the approach from there leads into the precincts. The above shrine buildings line the crest running east from the summit one level above the shrine-office plaza. ===Images=== Nisiteru shirine 03.jpg|Stone steps from the prefectural road to the precincts Nisiteru shirine 02.jpg|Main shrine haiden Nishiteru Jinja 05.JPG|Honden Nisiteru shirine 20240706 2.jpg|Kagura hall Nisiteru_jinja_20191217_02.jpg|Inari Shrine Nisiteruzinzya 01.jpg|Kumano Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 1.jpg|Hachidai Ryūō Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 2.jpg|Old komainu Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 3.jpg|Precincts Nisiteru 20250823 1.jpg|East entrance === References === {{No footnotes|date=August 2020|section=1}} * Tokushima Historical Society (ed.), ''New Edition: Historical Walks of Tokushima Prefecture'', Yamakawa Publishing, 1995, pp. 68-69. * On-site explanatory board === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commons|Category:Nishiteru-jinja}} *[http://nisiteru-jinja.com/ Mt. Ōtaki Nishiteru Shrine] - official site == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:美馬市の歴史]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:美馬市の建築物]]

Shrine}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '徳島県美馬市脇町西大谷672番地', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '徳島県三好郡東みよし町加茂', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Nishiteru-jinja}} [[JA:西照神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11629158}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Nishiteru Shrine | 画像=[[File:Nisiteru shirine 01.jpg|270px]]
Main shrine haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}Wakimachi Nishiotani 672 | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} | 創建=Unknown | 社格={{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭= | 本殿={{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Tokushima|label=Nishiteru Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Nishiteru Shrine'''（にしてるじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Wakimachi (Mima)|Q28689983|WD=Q28689983|lt=Wakimachi|1=Wakimachi}}, {{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}, {{ill|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|Q160734|WD=Q160734|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, and is also called "Tsukigami-no-miya" (Shrine of the Moon Deity). It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. Under the former {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. The main deity is Tsukuyomi Ōkami ({{ill|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}), with the three Munakata goddesses as associated deities: {{ill|lt=Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|WD=Q10932592|lt=Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagitsuhime}}, {{ill|lt=Takiribime|Q10932870|WD=Q10932870|lt=Tagorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tagorihime}}, and {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}. === Overview === It is located directly below the summit (946{{ill|lt=meter|Q11573|WD=Q11573|lt=m|1=Metre}}) of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki-yama (Sanuki Mountains)|Q11437573|WD=Q11437573|lt=Ōtaki-yama|1=Mount Otaki}}, on the border of {{ill|lt=Mima|Q857828|WD=Q857828|1=Mima, Tokushima}}, Tokushima Prefecture and {{ill|lt=Takamatsu|Q200707|WD=Q200707|1=Takamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is treated as a candidate shrine for Tajiki Shrine listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. According to shrine tradition, Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto, ruler of the land of night, dispatched Tajikitsuhime-no-Mikoto to Ōtaki-yama as an inspector toward {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495|1=Yamato Province}}, and from here had her watch over the {{ill|lt=Seto Inland Sea|Q231312|WD=Q231312|1=Seto Inland Sea}}; this is said to be the shrine's origin. It is said that in the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} practiced ascetic training on this mountain and installed Nishiteru Daigongen, making it a mountain training site of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=UNKNOWN}}. Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, it was called "Nishiteru Gongen." In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was venerated by the Inada clan, {{ill|lt=chief retainers|Q1316445|WD=Q1316445|1=Karō}} of the {{ill|lt=Tokushima Domain|Q3034557|WD=Q3034557|1=Tokushima Domain}}, who donated 9 chō 8 tan of shrine land, and by the lords of the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}}, who donated 50 koku for operating expenses as their prayer site. In the early Meiji era, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu bunri edict|1=UNKNOWN}} separated the shrine from its temple ({{ill|lt=Ōtaki-ji|Q11437672|WD=Q11437672|1=Otaki-ji Temple}}). In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was renamed Nishiteru Shrine and ranked as gōsha. * Legend of the komainu: The shrine has a {{ill|lt=komainu|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846|1=Komainu}} dedicated in {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}} 14 ({{ill|lt=1843|Q7629|WD=Q7629|1=1843}}). One summer night, a mother and child passing here were chased by a {{ill|lt=wolf|Q18498|WD=Q18498|lt=wolf|1=Wolf}} and fled into the shrine. When the wolf tried to attack them, the komainu drove it away. Due to age, the old statue was moved to a safe place under the honden, and a new pair now stands at the former position by the stone steps. ===Precincts=== * First torii: Beside the road; stone steps continue from here into the precincts. * Shrine office, kagura hall, and plaza * Second torii * Main shrine: haiden and honden * Inari Shrine: deity is Uga-no-Mitama Ōkami. Haiden and honden are combined in one structure. * Kumano Shrine: deities are Kumano Gongen, Izanagi Ōkami, and Izanami Ōkami. The Daigongen formerly enshrined at Okunoin of Mt. Ōtaki was moved here in early Shōwa. * Hachidai Ryūō Shrine: deities are Nanda, Upananda, Sāgara, Vāsuki, Takṣaka, Anavatapta, Manasvin, and Utpalaka. Stone hokora. * Prayer-stone area and votive horse painting "Tsuki no Mokuba" (painted by Fukuke Haruyo; completion ceremony 2019-11-10) * Tōmei cedar: height 50 m, said to be 500 years old and known as a "thousand-year cedar." Tradition says the tip shone nightly at the start of the First Sino-Japanese War and went dark at peace talks; a similar event is also said for the Russo-Japanese War. A parking lot is on the east pass side past Ōtaki-ji on Prefectural Route 106. A pair of komainu was installed at the east entrance in July 2025; walking about 50 m along the approach from there leads into the precincts. The above shrine buildings line the crest running east from the summit one level above the shrine-office plaza. ===Images=== Nisiteru shirine 03.jpg|Stone steps from the prefectural road to the precincts Nisiteru shirine 02.jpg|Main shrine haiden Nishiteru Jinja 05.JPG|Honden Nisiteru shirine 20240706 2.jpg|Kagura hall Nisiteru_jinja_20191217_02.jpg|Inari Shrine Nisiteruzinzya 01.jpg|Kumano Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 1.jpg|Hachidai Ryūō Shrine Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 2.jpg|Old komainu Nisiteru shrine 2024 917 3.jpg|Precincts Nisiteru 20250823 1.jpg|East entrance === References === {{No footnotes|date=August 2020|section=1}} * Tokushima Historical Society (ed.), ''New Edition: Historical Walks of Tokushima Prefecture'', Yamakawa Publishing, 1995, pp. 68-69. * On-site explanatory board === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commons|Category:Nishiteru-jinja}} *[http://nisiteru-jinja.com/ Mt. Ōtaki Nishiteru Shrine] - official site == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nishiteru Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Tokushima)]] [[Category:美馬市の歴史]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:美馬市の建築物]]
